title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 39:19-23 - GENESIS 40:1-23 Potiphar was furious when he heard his wife’s story about how Joseph had treated her. So he took Joseph and threw him into the prison where the king’s prisoners were held, and there he remained. But the LORD was with Joseph in the prison and showed him his faithful love. And the LORD made Joseph a favorite with the prison warden. Before long, the warden put Joseph in charge of all the other prisoners and over everything that happened in the prison. The warden had no more worries, because Joseph took care of everything. The LORD was with him and caused everything he did to succeed. Sometime later, Pharaoh’s chief cup-bearer and chief baker offended their royal master. Pharaoh became angry with these two officials, and he put them in the prison where Joseph was, in the palace of the captain of the guard. They remained in prison for quite some time, and the captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, who looked after them. While they were in prison, Pharaoh’s cup-bearer and baker each had a dream one night, and each dream had its own meaning. When Joseph saw them the next morning, he noticed that they both looked upset. “Why do you look so worried today?” he asked them. And they replied, “We both had dreams last night, but no one can tell us what they mean.” “Interpreting dreams is God’s business,” Joseph replied. “Go ahead and tell me your dreams.” So the chief cup-bearer told Joseph his dream first. “In my dream,” he said, “I saw a grapevine in front of me. The vine had three branches that began to bud and blossom, and soon it produced clusters of ripe grapes. I was holding Pharaoh’s wine cup in my hand, so I took a cluster of grapes and squeezed the juice into the cup. Then I placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand.”
weekTheme: Boys lose heart, men endure This week, we are looking at the difficulty of delays. Delays can be very discouraging because it can feel like we are stuck for ever. For young guys, there are so many delays to overcome. Developmental delays, where they don’t seem to learn as quickly as everyone else. Relational delays, where they struggle to make friends like everyone else. Spiritual delays, when they don’t understand the bible or faith and feel behind. In spite of the pain, delays are a part of the process of God forming and maturing us. Learning to handle delays is a key part of growing up. Understanding how God uses time is a key part of discipleship and development.

days.title: Patience With The Process
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:19-20 19 Potiphar was furious when he heard his wife’s story about how Joseph had treated her. 20 So he took Joseph and threw him into the prison where the king’s prisoners were held, and there he remained.
days.reflection: Joseph just can’t seem to catch a break. After resisting temptation and honoring God, he winds up in prison. But he doesn’t just spend the weekend there; he spent a while there. Verse 20 says these heavy words: There he remained. When Joseph was a teenager, God gave him a dream that he would be a great leader, but right now the dream seems to be in danger. The dream looks like it's dying. The dream is delayed. But although it may be very hard, Joseph is going to have to learn to have patience with the process. Yes, God will save Joseph. Yes, his purpose will come to pass. However, Joseph is going to have to learn to overcome the difficulty of delays with the power of patience. And he is going to learn this in prison. You live in a world that does not understand patience. You live in a time where people are used to getting things immediately. Think about your own life. If you are hungry, you go to the fridge and open the door and eat. Between hunger and satisfaction is only a few moments. If you want something on the internet, you can click a button and have it delivered the same day. If you want to play a video game, you turn on the machine and are instantly connected. To be honest, it’s kind of nice to get what we want instantly. It certainly feels good, but it actually has the effect of weakening us and keeping us in a state of immaturity. Boys want things immediately, but men have to learn patience. Think about a 2-year-old verses a 30-year-old. If a toddler doesn’t get what he wants, he throws a tantrum. Screaming, kicking, yelling, hitting. It’s embarrassing when you see a kid do this in public. A toddler will melt down if he doesn’t get a happy snack or the toy he sees at the checkout. Now, compare this to a 30-year-old man who understands the important of patience. He learns to control himself, work patiently without seeing immediate fruit, and is willing to wait to get what he wants. He can handle delays and setbacks and doesn’t lose heart. He realizes all good things take time and patience. Now imagine what you would think if you saw a 30-year-old man shopping with his wife, and when he doesn’t get a happy snack, he starts screaming and pouting and crying on the floor. You would immediately think it was inappropriate, and you would definitely lose respect for him. The world today is filled with grown men throwing tantrums on the floor of their lives. Many men act like babies when they have to wait for what they want. Joseph doesn’t throw a tantrum in prison. He simply trusted God while he learned to wait. God is making him the kind of person who can handle the dream God has for him. God will use the delays you face in your life to mature you into a powerful, patient man. After all, it’s better to be respected than throw a tantrum in the moment.
questions: Ask the person you are doing this study with if they ever saw you throw a tantrum when you were little. Where was it, and why did you throw it?
The dictionary gives this definition for patience: “the calm endurance of hardship, provocation, pain, delay, etc.” Do you consider yourself a patient person? Why or why now?
Can you give an example that’s shows you were patient?
What have you learned from Joseph's life so far that makes you think you can be patient when you face the difficulty of delays?

thoughts.verse: Better to be patient than powerful; better to have self-control than to conquer a city.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you power to grow in patience. If there is a specific situation you are facing right now that requires perseverance, ask him for help in that.

days.title: Delays And Disappointment
days.scripture: GENESIS 40:1-23 1 Sometime later, Pharaoh’s chief cup-bearer and chief baker offended their royal master. 2 Pharaoh became angry with these two officials, 3 and he put them in the prison where Joseph was, in the palace of the captain of the guard. 4 They remained in prison for quite some time, and the captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, who looked after them. 5 While they were in prison, Pharaoh’s cup-bearer and baker each had a dream one night, and each dream had its own meaning. 6 When Joseph saw them the next morning, he noticed that they both looked upset. 7 “Why do you look so worried today?” he asked them. 8 And they replied, “We both had dreams last night, but no one can tell us what they mean.” “Interpreting dreams is God’s business,” Joseph replied. “Go ahead and tell me your dreams.” 9 So the chief cup-bearer told Joseph his dream first. “In my dream,” he said, “I saw a grapevine in front of me. 10 The vine had three branches that began to bud and blossom, and soon it produced clusters of ripe grapes. 11 I was holding Pharaoh’s wine cup in my hand, so I took a cluster of grapes and squeezed the juice into the cup. Then I placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand.” 12 “This is what the dream means,” Joseph said. “The three branches represent three days. 13 Within three days Pharaoh will lift you up and restore you to your position as his chief cup-bearer. 14 And please remember me and do me a favor when things go well for you. Mention me to Pharaoh, so he might let me out of this place. 15 For I was kidnapped from my homeland, the land of the Hebrews, and now I’m here in prison, but I did nothing to deserve it.” 16 When the chief baker saw that Joseph had given the first dream such a positive interpretation, he said to Joseph, “I had a dream, too. In my dream there were three baskets of white pastries stacked on my head. 17 The top basket contained all kinds of pastries for Pharaoh, but the birds came and ate them from the basket on my head.”18 “This is what the dream means,” Joseph told him. “The three baskets also represent three days. 19 Three days from now Pharaoh will lift you up and impale your body on a pole. Then birds will come and peck away at your flesh.” 20 Pharaoh’s birthday came three days later, and he prepared a banquet for all his officials and staff. He summoned his chief cup-bearer and chief baker to join the other officials. 21 He then restored the chief cup-bearer to his former position, so he could again hand Pharaoh his cup. 22 But Pharaoh impaled the chief baker, just as Joseph had predicted when he interpreted his dream. 23 Pharaoh’s chief cup-bearer, however, forgot all about Joseph, never giving him another thought.
days.reflection: What happens next while Joseph is in prison is both weird and wonderful. It’s also kind of painful. Because Joseph is in the royal prison, important people are thrown in there. Because Joseph has favor from God, these important people are assigned to his care. These men have troubling dreams but they don’t know how to interpret them. Joseph realizes that God has given him the ability to understand the dreams, and he shares what they mean to these men. As you read in this passage, what Joseph shared came true. One was killed, and the other restored. This is incredible. This is exciting. Joseph must be thinking, “This is my moment! This is how I am going to get out of here." You can see this in what he says in verse 14 -15, “And please remember me and do me a favor when things go well for you. Mention me to Pharaoh, so he might let me out of this place. For I was kidnapped from my homeland, the land of the Hebrews, and now I’m here in prison, but I did nothing to deserve it.” If ever it looked like a divine setup, this is it. You think the story is about to wrap up, but you probably guessed it by now... ANOTHER DELAY. Verse 23 has these tragic words: “Pharaoh’s chief cup-bearer, however, forgot all about Joseph, never giving him another thought.” What a beatdown for Joseph. Another door is closed, and he is rotting in prison. This must have been really hard to handle. Proverbs 13:12 says: "Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a dream fulfilled is a tree of life." Delays cause disappointment. It’s something all young men must realize and confront. It is hard when our plans don’t work out; it is hard when things we hope for don’t materialize; it’s hard when things get moved around, pushed off, cancelled or changed. Part of learning to be a man is learning to deal with disappointment in our plans. We have 3 responses in our disappointment: Blame. You can get mad at God, mad at your friends, mad at your parents, or mad at your teachers. It's ok to feel frustration and experience disappointment, but getting mad and angry doesn’t change the actual outcome. After the initial frustration, you have to calm yourself down and move forward or you may get stuck in bitterness, which you don’t want in your life. Sulk. You can pout around feeling sorry for yourself and being miserable. Again, it's ok to be hurt and sad, but you don’t want to get stuck there. After acknowledging that things hurt, you have to come out of the sulking towards a solution. This takes REAL maturity, but God can really help you here if you ask him. Trust. You can believe that even though this isn’t turning out how you want, God has not forgotten you. He has the ability to turn this around for good. He can use this time to strengthen you. He will work his purpose out for you eventually. Joseph had to learn yet again to get his eyes off the problem and on to a person. He had to shift his attention from what was going on around him to what God was doing in him. When he did this, he lived out what the Psalmist talked about in Psalm 27:14: Wait for the Lord; Be strong and let your heart take courage; Yes, wait for the Lord. Share your disappointment with God, but don’t get stuck there. Keep going. God worked things out for Joseph, and he will for you too. He is with you and for you, and that can be a source of comfort, even though things hurt.
questions: How do you normally act when you are disappointed? Blame, sulk, or trust?
Are you willing to share a time when you have been really disappointed? What was it, and why did it matter to you?
Ask the person doing this study with you to share how they have learned to handle disappointments. Ask them to share a story that shows this.

thoughts.verse: For God is pleased with you when you do what you know is right and patiently endure unfair treatment. Of course, you get no credit for being patient if you are beaten for doing wrong. But if you suffer for doing good and endure it patiently, God is pleased with you.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to sit before God and be quiet, and think about something that is disappointing in your life right now. Bring this before God. Ask for his help, and share your feelings with him. Be honest, even if it feels weird. He really does care about all the things happening in your life.

days.title: Delays And Fomo
days.scripture: GENESIS 40:14 And please remember me and do me a favor when things go well for you. Mention me to Pharaoh, so he might let me out of this place.
days.reflection: You’ve probably heard of the term FOMO before. Fear. Of. Missing. Out. It’s one of the great tensions in a man’s heart that drives a lot of his behavior. No one likes missing out, especially if you feel like everybody else is having fun and you are the one person sitting on the sideline. The people who run our world today understand the power of using FOMO to manipulate our behavior. FOMO is used to get you to buy stuff in the spur of the moment, nag your parents for stuff you think you need, or do dumb things with your friends without thinking. The FEAR of missing out is a very real thing we have to confront. You can see a bit of this fear with Joseph. He was worried he would be in prison forever. In the verse from today’s reading, there is a desperation in his request. A sense that he is stuck and going to be left behind if something doesn’t happen. This is a natural instinct and one we all feel. But Joseph is going to learn a really important lesson here. His future doesn’t depend on favors from others or even help from Pharaoh. His future depends on God. The truth is, Joseph isn’t missing out on anything. God's timing is perfect, and he will learn to see this later on. You will have times when you feel like you are missing out on things others are getting to do. But if you follow Jesus and see the delays as opportunities for growth, amazing things will happen later on. Here is the good news: if you surrender your life to God, you will never miss out on the stuff that REALLY matters. You may miss out on some stuff, but not the most important stuff God has for you. Paul said in Philippians 1:6, “And I am certain that God, who began the good work within you, will continue his work until it is finally finished on the day when Christ Jesus returns.” God has started something in you, and he will continue to work his plans out for your life. He will help you figure out where or if to go to college and what to study, who to marry, what to do for a job, where you should live - all the big things. He will guide the timing and testing of your life to work out his plan. This is really good news, because it means someone is there making sure the most important stuff you need for your life will happen. Don’t panic if you feel like you are missing out. You are being formed, not forgotten, just like Joseph was.
questions: Have you ever experienced FOMO? Is there anything happening with your friends or kids at school right now that you feel like you may be missing out on?
Do you see any of your friends struggling with FOMO? If so, about what?
Ask the person doing this study with you to share about what they were worried about missing out on when they were your age, and how it all turned out.

thoughts.verse: I waited patiently for the LORD to help me, and he turned to me and heard my cry. He lifted me out of the pit of despair, out of the mud and the mire. He set my feet on solid ground and steadied me as I walked along. He has given me a new song to sing, a hymn of praise to our God. Many will see what he has done and be amazed. They will put their trust in the LORD.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to take away any fears you may have on missing out on things that really matter to you. Ask him to show you his faithful love.

days.title: Redeeming Delays
days.scripture: GENESIS 39:21:23 But the LORD was with Joseph in the prison and showed him his faithful love. And the LORD made Joseph a favorite with the prison warden. Before long, the warden put Joseph in charge of all the other prisoners and over everything that happened in the prison. The warden had no more worries, because Joseph took care of everything. The LORD was with him and caused everything he did to succeed.
days.reflection: One of the greatest challenges in dealing with the difficulties of delay is that we feel like time is being wasted. It’s hard to fight off discouragement when we feel like we aren’t making any progress. Joseph is in prison, and it doesn’t look like he will be going anyplace else anytime soon. Yet, something amazing happens. He chooses to use his time wisely and redeem the delay. Joseph works and waits, waits and works, and God gives him favor. Living inside of time is one of the weirdest things about being a human. We can remember the past, dream about the future, but we can only live the moment. How we view time is actually a really important part of growing into adulthood. Time is one of the most important gifts that you have, so you can’t afford to waste a season where you feel stuck doing nothing. Think about this. The average person sleeps for 26 years of their life. The average person will spend 7 years of their life trying to fall asleep. The average person spends 5 years waiting in traffic. The average person spends 1.5 years of their life on the toilet. (Yep. True) The average person spends 118 days looking for stuff they have lost. The average person spends 11 years and 4 months looking at screens. The average person spends 115 days of their life laughing. You can redeem the moments you have or you can waste them. You can be frustrated or you can be fruitful. It’s all about your attitude and your ability to trust God. At the end of the day, your life is simply about how you choose to use the time you have. Joseph had to deal with a delay in his destiny, and yet he used his time in prison to get closer to God and work toward his calling. Joseph knew he was meant to be a leader, so he led wherever he found himself. In slavery, he was a leader. In prison, he was a leader. And the same things he is learning now will help him later. Don’t waste your delay time. Be present and learn and grow where you are. Don’t fall into the trap of thinking things will be better if you were in a different season. In middle school, you may think, “I can’t wait to get to high school.” In high school, “I can’t wait to get to college." In college, “I can’t wait to get married.” When married, “I can’t wait to have kids.” When you have kids, “I can’t wait to have a home.” None of this helps you become the right person in the next season. Worry about now. Jesus said in Matthew 6:31-33 “So don’t worry about these things, saying, ‘What will we eat? What will we drink? What will we wear?’ These things dominate the thoughts of unbelievers, but your heavenly Father already knows all your needs. Seek the Kingdom of God above all else, and live righteously, and he will give you everything you need.” Instead of wishing you were in another situation or worrying you will miss out, work in the season you are in. Use the time; don’t waste the time. When you walk with God, nothing is wasted. Even delays can be redeemed if they help you grow.
questions: What is the longest you have ever had to wait for something? What was it, and why did it take so long?
When you get bored or discouraged, what do you tend to do with your time? Is this helpful for your future, or is it just wasted in the moment? Why do you think that matters?
What is one lesson you may be able to learn from this stage of your life that you think could help you in the next one?

thoughts.verse: But you must not forget this one thing, dear friends: A day is like a thousand years to the Lord, and a thousand years is like a day. The Lord isn’t really being slow about his promise, as some people think. No, he is being patient for your sake. He does not want anyone to be destroyed, but wants everyone to repent.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you understand the importance of time and using your time well. Ask him to show you how he might be preparing you now, even when things seem to be dragging along or taking forever.

days.title: Do Not Lose Heart
days.scripture: GENESIS 40:6-7 6 When Joseph saw them the next morning, he noticed that they both looked upset. 7 “Why do you look so worried today?” he asked them.
days.reflection: “Hurry up.” “I can't wait.” “This is taking forever.” It's crazy how much of our language is about trying to get things to happen faster. How we view time and deal with time seems to show up everywhere. If you go back and read this section of scripture from Joseph's life, you will see how much of this story is driven by the idea of time as well. Here is just a little sample: “Before long” “And there he remained” “Sometime later” “They remained in prison for quite some time” “In three days” “Never giving him another thought” Learning to deal with delays is so important. Being patient, not losing heart, hanging in there, and learning to trust God when things look bleak are some of the keys to manhood. These are the things Joseph had to learn at this part of his life and the things you are going to have to learn to really grow and not get stuck in immaturity. In general, people in the Bible had to wait a while for the promises of God to be fulfilled. Abraham had to wait almost 25 years for the kid God promised him to show up. Joseph is going to have to wait twenty years before his father finally shows up and bows down before him like in the dream he had as a teenager. David was a teenager when Samuel anointed him as king, but he didn’t start to reign in Israel until he was 30 years old. Even Jesus spent 30 years behind the scenes doing a regular job before he moved into his public ministry. Understanding that this is normally the way God works can help you keep going when you feel like the things you want are delayed. It gives us perspective when we are tempted to quit. That’s why Galatians 6:9 says: “So let’s not get tired of doing what is good. At just the right time we will reap a harvest of blessing if we don’t give up.” At just the right time. God knows what he is doing. He works stuff out in amazing ways. That’s why we shouldn’t give up in the middle of things. God is working, even if it seems slow. Paul prayed in the New Testament that followers of Jesus “will be strengthened with all his glorious power so you will have all the endurance and patience you need.“ (Colossians 1:11) He prayed for power to hang in there. It’s a great thing for you to pray too. So don’t quit. Winston Churchill once said, "If you are going through hell, keep going." What if David had given up? Or Jesus had given into the devil’s temptations in the wilderness? Or Joseph stopped bothering to be faithful to God because he got discouraged. Ecclesiastes 3:1 and 3:11 says, “For everything there is a season, a time for every activity under heaven. Yet God has made everything beautiful for its own time.” Good things come to those who wait; God things come to those who wait too. Stay the course.
questions: If you could fast forward your life to a point when you are older, when would that be and why?
If you could go back in time to a moment when you were younger, when would it be and why?

thoughts.verse: I wait quietly before God, for my victory comes from him. He alone is my rock and my salvation, my fortress where I will never be shaken.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you have courage to obey him no matter what the cost. Ask him specifically for any challenge you may be having right now.

extras: WEEK 5 MANSCHOOL BROWNIES WITH A TWIST OPTION 1. BAKING BROWNIES AND WAITING. Men should be able to cook stuff. Zero apologies here. This is brownies with a twist. The goal is to teach patience with the process. Get a brownie mix and all ingredients. (Get some weird candies you can throw in there too to mix it up.) Mix all the ingredients. Put it in the oven. When the brownies go in the oven, sit in front of them and wait. The whole time. This should take about 30 minutes. Make a deal not to leave the table or chair in front of the oven till they are ready. (This will be excruciatingly hard for both of you) Then, when they are ready, set them in front of you for 10 minutes to cool down. No samples allowed. When ready, eat them and talk about what you learned. How hard was the process of waiting? What did you learning about yourself? OPTION 2. WATCH Watch RUDY, the classic film about perseverance, determination and delays. If your son isn’t into sports, watch BACK TO THE FUTURE and focus on the importance and wonder of time. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Go out to one of your sons’ favorite restaurants and recap the week. You can talk through the big ideas of the week and see what resonated with him:
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
